perturbation.original_text: 15. PAYMENT TERMS The Distributor agrees to abide by Lucid's standard payment terms, which are as follows; One Hundred (100) per cent of order value by sixty ( 90 ) day irrevocable letter of credit when order is placed on Lucid.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. PAYMENT TERMS The Distributor agrees to abide by Lucid's standard payment terms, which are as follows; One Hundred (100) per cent of order value upon delivery of product.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 'One Hundred (100) per cent of order value upon delivery of product' without an irrevocable letter of credit or other security arrangement may contradict Article 2 of the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) regarding payment and acceptance of goods, potentially increasing risk for the seller without proper mitigation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original text specifies payment via a 90-day irrevocable letter of credit, providing security. The modified text demands full payment upon delivery, which, without the security of a letter of credit, alters the payment risk and contradicts the reasonable commercial standards often implied under UCC Article 2.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-310. Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-310. Open Time for Payment or Running of Credit; Authority to Ship Under Reservation. Unless otherwise agreed (a) payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the goods even though the place of shipment is the place of delivery; and (b) if the seller is authorized to send the goods he may ship them under reservation, and may tender the documents of title, but the buyer may inspect the goods after their arrival before payment is due unless such inspection is inconsistent with the terms of the contract (Section 2-513 ); and (c) if delivery is authorized and made by way of documents of title otherwise than by subsection (b) then payment is due at the time and place at which the buyer is to receive the documents regardless of where the goods are to be received; and (d) where the seller is required or authorized to ship the goods on credit the credit period runs from the time of shipment but post-dating the invoice or delaying its dispatch will correspondingly delay the starting of the credit period . Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a letter of credit for payment security, whereas the changed contract requires payment upon delivery without mentioning any security. This change contradicts reasonable commercial standards, which is implied under UCC Article 2, as explained in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from UCC § 2-310, states payment is due at the time the buyer receives the goods 'unless otherwise agreed.' While 'unless otherwise agreed' offers contractual flexibility, removing a letter of credit entirely shifts risk substantially without a clear agreement addressing this risk, potentially violating commercial standards related to payment security. Given the explicit removal of the security of the letter of credit, it creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 21. NOTICE A) All notices required herein ("NOTICE") shall be given in English language. Notices must be sent postage pre-paid, and (a) delivered personally against written receipt (b) transmitted via facsimile or (c) sent by nationally recognised overnight courier service, or (d) sent via first class certified mail, return receipt requested, to the address listed above. Any change of addresser fax number must be designated in writing and served in accordance with this Section. Notice shall be effective (a) upon receipt if personally delivered (b) upon confirmation of transmission if sent via facsimile and (c) two (2) business days after deposit with the courier or an official depository of the US Post Office, if sent via recognised overnight courier of via Certified Mail, as the case may be.
perturbation.changed_text: 21. NOTICE A) All notices required herein ("NOTICE") shall be given in any language. Notices must be sent without postage pre-payment, and (a) delivered personally without written receipt (b) transmitted via telepathy or (c) sent by unrecognised overnight courier service, or (d) sent via third class certified mail, without return receipt requested, to the address listed above. Any change of addresser fax number must be designated orally and served by shouting it from the roof. Notice shall be effective (a) upon telepathic receipt if personally delivered (b) upon denial of transmission if sent via telepathy and (c) ten (10) business days after deposit with the courier or an official depository of the US Post Office, if sent via unrecognised overnight courier of via Certified Mail, as the case may be.
perturbation.explanation: The changes to the notice clause such as removing the English language requirement, postage pre-payment, written receipt for delivery, utilizing telepathy, unrecognised couriers, and oral change of address notification introduces ambiguity and impracticality, making it nearly impossible to prove proper notice was given. This can conflict with basic contract law principles requiring reasonable notice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law Principles of 'Reasonable Notice'
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification of the notice clause to include methods such as 'telepathy' and 'unrecognised courier services' creates an unreasonable and unenforceable standard. This contradicts the common law principle that contract terms must be clear, definite, and capable of objective proof, and therefore, a court would likely deem this clause unenforceable. Clear and reasonable notice is essential for the fair execution of a contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: consideration | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you consideration Consideration is a promise , performance, or forbearance bargained by a promisor in exchange for their promise. Consideration is the main element of a contract . Without consideration by both parties, a contract cannot be enforceable. For instance, if a person used the money to purchase an apple, the apple is the merchant’s consideration, and the money is the person’s consideration. Types: Consideration could be a promise, performance, forbearance, or property with legal value, but the economic benefit is not required. A gift or gratuitous promise cannot be a consideration for they have no bargaining. The past performance also cannot be a consideration as there is no exchange. Substitute for consideration: A contract without consideration could be enforceable if it has a substitute. Substitutes are promissory estoppel or detrimental reliance under Restatement (Second) of Contracts , or good faith modification under UCC . Promissory estoppel /detrimental reliance: A contract without consideration is enforceable if the nonperformance of the promisor will cause injustice. Elements of promissory estoppel are (i) the promise has reasonable, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance on the promisor, and (ii) the enforcement of the promise is necessary to avoid injustice. Good faith modification: A modified contract is a kind of new agreement, which changes parties’ obligations and then requires new consideration. But contract modification made in good faith under UCC is enforceable even without consideration. [Last reviewed in July of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces several elements (telepathy, unrecognised couriers, oral notifications) that are impractical and lack objective proof, making the notice clause unenforceable. The Law Explanation states that contract terms must be clear, definite, and capable of objective proof, a standard that the modified clause fails to meet. The modification directly contradicts the principle that notice must be reasonable and capable of verification, leading to a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Term A) The initial term of this Agreement shall be three (3) calendar years from the Agreement date. After the initial term, unless terminated, this Agreement will automatically renew for periods of one (1) calendar year each.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Term A) The initial term of this Agreement shall be three (3) calendar years from the Agreement date. After the initial term, unless terminated, this Agreement will automatically renew for periods of one hundred (100) calendar years each.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the automatic renewal period to 100 years could be challenged under the principle of 'unconscionability' in contract law, particularly if one party is significantly disadvantaged by such a long-term commitment. Courts may view such a term as unduly oppressive or unfair.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original agreement had a 1-year auto-renewal clause. Increasing that to 100 years can be unconscionable. UCC § 2-302 allows a court to refuse to enforce a contract (or part of it) deemed unconscionable at the time it was made. A 100-year renewal is very likely to be deemed unconscionable due to the vast disparity in commitment. This introduces a legal challenge to the term's enforceability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the auto-renewal period from 1 year to 100 years. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest this could be deemed unconscionable under UCC § 2-302. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-302, states that a court may refuse to enforce a contract or clause found to be unconscionable at the time it was made. A 100-year renewal period is likely to be viewed as unduly oppressive, thus creating a legal contradiction based on unconscionability.
